text: The surgeon uses three ports on the left side, including a submemory incision and one in the axilla, to access the anterior mediastinum. The mediastinal pleura is opened anterior to the left phrenic nerve, revealing a lump on the left thymic lobe. The surgeon carefully dissects around the lump inferiorly, lifting it and opening the ionic capsule off the fibrous pericardium to release the mass.

The surgeon dissects the thymic mass from underneath, carefully lifting it off the pulmonary trunk and the ascending and arch of the aorta, separating it from the great vessels emerging from the heart.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the blood vessels, including the vein and artery draining into and out of the left internal mammary, taking care to avoid injury to the adjacent left phrenic nerve.
The video shows a visually aggressive and invasive tumor in the anterior mediastinum, likely the thymoma previously identified.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the innominate vein, establishing a clear surgical plane while avoiding injury to surrounding structures.
The surgeon retracts the left biomic horn, which extends into the neck, following its path up to the thyroid gland.

The surgeon uses a hook to support the thymocorne and employs a bipolar fenestrated instrument to drag down, revealing the vessels at the top of the thymocorne originating from the thyroid. The vessels are clipped for safety before using diathermy to divide them.
The surgeon releases tissue on the right side, approaching the confluence of the innominate veins into the superior vena cava (SVC).
The surgeon carefully dissects around large vessels, using a Hemolock clip for secure vessel management.

The surgeon isolates the vein draining into the superior vena cava (SVC) while carefully avoiding injury to the right phrenic nerve, which descends from the right internal mammary base along the lateral side of the SVC. Blunt dissection is employed to achieve this separation.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node and uses a bipolar device to safely divide it, ensuring no electrical conduction to the nearby phrenic nerve.
The surgeon opens the right pleura to enhance visualization, dividing the remaining tissue. An endocatch bag is used to prevent any sealing in the left pleura.
The surgeon inserts an endocatch bag through a twelve-millimeter port in the axilla, closes the bag, and pulls it through the port.
The surgeon displays a clear view of the innominate artery and aorta, free of surrounding tissue, followed by a comparative image showing the area before and after tumor resection.
